text:
Your description reminds me of a <https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Schmitt_trigger|Schmitt trigger> used in electronics. One can implement a Schmitt-trigger-like action in the digital domain with a state machine:
S1: when a 0->1 event occurs, go to state S2.
S2: when a 1->0 event occurs go back to state S1.
(I think of the timer variant as a Schmitt trigger that deals with analog signals).

text: I have a problem shaped a lot like this in my “turn-orchestrator” for my agent harness. I’m tempted to solve it with timers (like “if no activity for 15 seconds, then it’s time to mark this as idled”), but it makes me itchy to solve a problem like that.

elements.text: I have a problem shaped a lot like this in my “turn-orchestrator” for my agent harness. I’m tempted to solve it with timers (like “if no activity for 15 seconds, then it’s time to mark this as idled”), but it makes me itchy to solve a problem like that.

text: I don’t know how the AI solved it at the end of the day. But, it’s not using timers and it’s probably inspired by temporal .io

text:
Great write-up.

I might be missing a realization — what makes this "inhibitory" approach different from a "throttle"? ("debounce" is also similar, maybe that's what i mean?)

elements.text:
Great write-up.

I might be missing a realization — what makes this "inhibitor
" approach different from a "throttle"? ("debounce" is also similar, maybe that's what i mean?)

text: Yes - it's exactly that! A throttle (or some variant of throttle - I'm also not familiar with precise terminology around those concepts). The thing is that this throttle can be used to prevent back-flow of information in reactive graphs. This allows reactive graphs with cycles & multidirectional dataflow - which are otherwise finnicky and prone to oscillations to behave in a more robust fashion.
